title: L01.3.basics
body:
Toolkit for Finance and Financial Decision Making
Lecture 1.3: Python Basics

Breno Schmidt and Robert Tumarkin
UNSW




                                                    1
 Python Basics
 Python Basics: Execution order




 Python executes code from the top of the file to the bottom, one line at a time.

      This is called the flow of execution or control flow.
      Statements run immediately as they are encountered, in order.
      The program ends when there are no more statements to execute.




                                                                                    2
 Python: Variables and assignment statements




 We attach a variable (name) to an memory location (object) using an assignment statement


 Assignment statements have the form:
                                    variable_name = expression


 On execution, Python:

   1. Checks that variable_name is a valid name
   2. Evaluates the expression
   3. Assigns the instance produced by the expression to variable_name.




                                                                                            3
 Python Basics: White Space




 White space (space, tab, and end-of-line characters) is syntactical in Python


 Statements indented at the same level form code blocks. For the first two weeks, we deal
 with the single, outermost code block:
      All statements must be left aligned with no leading spaces.
      Blank lines are permitted.


                                   We will return to this topic in week 3.




                                                                                            4
 Python Basics: Comments and blank lines




 Comments are useful to explain the logic behind your code – why this particular structure is used

      Comments start with a # symbol and extend to the end of the line.
      Python ignores everything after # on that line.
      There are two types of comments:
         Single-line comments: Begin with # and are used for short explanations.
         Inline comments: Placed at the end of a line of code, separated by at least two spaces from the
            code.


 Blank lines are used to visually separate sections:

      Blank lines are ignored by Python.
      Separating code with blank lines creates visual segments of code, making the visual
      structure reflect the programmatic structure.




                                                                                                            5
 Python Basics: Printing




    Printing is one way for us to get information from our programs:

1       print("Hello from Python")
2       print(1)
3       print(2.0)
4       print(True)
5       print('Goodbye from Python')



    A basic print statement consists of:
         The function print
         An object surrounded by parentheses




                                                                       6
 Python Basics: Printing




    Printing information along with objects requires an f-String:

1       x = 1
2       print(f'x is {x}')
3       print(f"x is {x}")



    An f-String consists of:
         The f character
         Text surrounded by either single- or double-quotes
         A message with any objects surrounded by curly braces




                                                                    7
 Python Basics: Exceptions




 Exceptions are error events that disrupt the normal flow of a program.

 When an exception occurs, Python checks if the program is prepared to handle the exception:

      If so, Python runs code in an attempt to recover
      If not, Python stops execution and gives you an error message with information about
      what caused the exception


                              We will focus on understanding error messages.




                                                                                               8
 Python Basics: Exceptions


    Let’s examine an exception in detail:
1         File "/home/main.py", line 1
2           2x = 1
3           ˆ
4       SyntaxError: invalid syntax



    It is often helpful to read error messages from the bottom up:
         The bottom line indicates the type of exception that was raised.
             SyntaxError: invalid syntax: Indicates a violation of the syntax rules (i.e., starting a variable
               name with a number).

         The beginning of the error message provides the location.
             In File “/home/main.py”, line 1.
             As Python “read” the statement 2x = 1
             Indicated by the “caret” (ˆ) pointing to the precise location in the statement.



    Python has numerous exceptions. Parsing them carefully from the bottom up will allow you to correct your
                                                    code.



                                                                                                                  9
